metadata.summary: Core unit test principles: FIRST, AAA pattern, naming conventions, coverage strategies, and anti-patterns.

content.title: Unit Testing
content.summary: Covers the FIRST properties (Fast/Isolated/Repeatable/Self-validating/Timely), Arrange-Act-Assert structure, test naming conventions (method-scenario-expected, should-when, given-when-then), coverage strategies (line vs branch vs mutation), test categories, and the most damaging anti-patterns (testing implementation, not behavior).

why.p: Unit tests are the foundation of every test pyramid. Without consistent structure and naming, suites become maintenance burdens: tests that pass for wrong reasons, fail for infrastructure reasons, or describe nothing useful in their names. FIRST + AAA enforces a minimal discipline that scales.

list.item: Writing the first unit tests for a function, method, or class
Reviewing unit test quality (FIRST compliance, AAA structure, naming)
Choosing a coverage strategy for a new module
Identifying test anti-patterns: testing internals, slow unit tests, non-isolated fixtures
Onboarding new developers to the project test style
pytest-specific features (fixtures, parametrize) — use testing-pytest
Mocking strategy decisions — use mocking-strategies
E2E tests — use e2e-testing
Test fixture design — use test-fixtures
Fast — unit tests must run in milliseconds. No I/O, no network, no sleep.
Isolated — each test sets up its own state and does not depend on other tests or global state.
Repeatable — produces identical results on any machine, any time, any order.
Self-validating — has a binary pass/fail outcome. No manual inspection of logs.
Timely — written at the same time as (or before) the production code they test.

rule.statement: If a test violates any FIRST property, it is not a unit test — it is an integration or system test.
Structure every test with a blank line separating Arrange, Act, and Assert sections.
One test = one Act. Multiple assertions on the result of a single Act are acceptable.

example.title: AAA structure example
Multiple acts in one test
example.description: Multiple acts in one test — splits into two separate tests
example.code: def test_cart(): cart = ShoppingCart() cart.add_item(price=100.0) assert len(cart.items) == 1 # act + assert 1 cart.add_item(price=50.0) assert len(cart.items) == 2 # act + assert 2 — should be a separate test

rule.statement: Name tests to describe what they test, not how they implement it.

list.item: method_scenario_expected: add_item_when_duplicate_increments_quantity
should_behavior_when_condition: should_raise_when_price_is_negative
given_when_then: given_premium_user_when_checkout_then_discount_applied

rule.statement: Test class names: TestClassName (groups tests for the same class).
Test function names must start with test_ for pytest discovery.
A unit is the smallest piece of behavior that can be tested in isolation.
A unit is NOT always a single function — it can be a cluster of collaborating classes if they form a coherent behavior.
Replace external dependencies (I/O, time, randomness) with test doubles. Keep internal collaborators real.

list.item: Line coverage — measures whether each line was executed. Lowest bar, easiest to game.
Branch coverage — measures whether each branch (if/else, switch) was taken in both directions. More meaningful.
Mutation coverage — measures whether tests detect deliberate code mutations. Highest confidence, most expensive.

rule.statement: Aim for branch coverage, not line coverage. A function with 100% line coverage but untested edge cases is still fragile.

list.item: Business logic / domain layer: 90%+ branch coverage.
API handlers / controllers: 80%+ line coverage.
Infrastructure / adapters: 60%+ (often tested at integration level).
Generated code / migrations: exclude from coverage entirely.

rule.statement: Set cov-fail-under in CI — enforce minimums, but do not treat high coverage as a goal in itself.

antipattern.reason: Coverage measures execution, not correctness. Always assert observable outcomes.

antipattern.description: Testing internals to reach coverage targets
antipattern.reason: Private methods and implementation details should be tested through public API. If a private method is complex enough to need direct testing, extract it to a separate class.

rule.statement: Use mutation testing (mutmut, pitest) on critical business logic to validate that your tests actually catch errors.

antipattern.description: Asserting on private methods or internal state
antipattern.code:
def test_bad(): svc = OrderService() svc.process(order) assert svc._internal_cache["order_123"] == order # tests internal detail
def test_good(): svc = OrderService() result = svc.process(order) assert result.status == "confirmed" # tests observable behavior
antipattern.reason: Tests tied to implementation break on every refactor, even when behavior is correct.

antipattern.description: Asserting on floating-point equality without tolerance
antipattern.code: assert total == 0.1 + 0.2 # fails: 0.30000000000000004 assert abs(total - 0.3) < 1e-9 # correct
antipattern.reason: Floating-point arithmetic is not exact — use pytest.approx() or math.isclose().

antipattern.description: Asserting on dictionary ordering or set iteration order
antipattern.reason: Python dicts are ordered by insertion since 3.7, but set ordering is undefined. Use sorted() for sets, explicit key lookups for dicts.

antipattern.description: Mocking every dependency in a unit test, including simple value objects
antipattern.reason: When the test mocks everything, it proves nothing. Use real collaborators within your own codebase; only mock external boundaries (I/O, time, network).

antipattern.description: Unit test touches the filesystem, database, or network
antipattern.reason: Unit tests must run in milliseconds. Use tmp_path for files, in-memory SQLite for DB, mocks for network. Slow unit tests → developers skip running them locally.

antipattern.description: Tests share a mutable object created once in conftest or setUp
antipattern.reason: Shared mutable state makes tests order-dependent and non-repeatable.
